revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Towatari Shrine
登渡神社 | image = Towatari-jinja_06.jpg | caption = Towatari Shrine's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|36|44.3|N|140|6|22.6|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (formerly [[Myōken]])
[[Kamimusubi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]] | founded_by = [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]] | established = [[1644 (year)|1644]] ([[Shōhō]] 1) | date_destroyed = | location = 3-3-8, Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.towatarijinjya.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = 4–6 September | leadership = }} '''Towatari Shrine''' (登渡神社, ''Towatari-jinja''), also known as '''Nobuto Shrine''' (登戸神社, ''Nobuto-jinja''), is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *[https://www.towatarijinjya.com/ Official website of Towatari Shrine] (Japanese) [[category:17th-century Shinto shrines]] [[category:1600s establishments in Japan]] [[category:Buildings and structures in Chiba (city)]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]‎]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Myōken Faith]]{{Myōken Faith}}[[category:Myōken Faith]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Towatari Shrine
登渡神社 | image = Towatari-jinja_06.jpg | caption = Towatari Shrine's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|36|44.3|N|140|6|22.6|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (formerly [[Myōken]])
[[Kamimusubi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]] | founded_by = [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]] | established = [[1644 (year)|1644]] ([[Shōhō]] 1) | date_destroyed = | location = 3-3-8, Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.towatarijinjya.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = 4–6 September | leadership = }} '''Towatari Shrine''' (登渡神社, ''Towatari-jinja''), also known as '''Nobuto Shrine''' (登戸神社, ''Nobuto-jinja''), is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *[https://www.towatarijinjya.com/ Official website of Towatari Shrine] (Japanese) [[category:17th-century Shinto shrines]] [[category:1600s establishments in Japan]] [[category:Buildings and structures in Chiba (city)]] [[category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]‎]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[category:mm]] [[category:Myōken Faith]]{{Myōken Faith}}[[category:Myōken Faith]] [[category:mass editing for the third subcategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Kamimusubi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]] | founded_by = [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]] | established = [[1644 (year)|1644]] ([[Shōhō]] 1) | date_destroyed = | location = 3-3-8, Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.towatarijinjya.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = 4–6 September | leadership = }} '''Towatari Shrine''' (登渡神社, ''Towatari-jinja''), also known as '''Nobuto Shrine''' (登戸神社, ''Nobuto-jinja''), is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *[https://www.towatarijinjya.com/ Official website of Towatari Shrine] (Japanese) [[Category:17th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:1600s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiba (city)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Myōken Faith]]{{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:Myōken Faith]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Towatari Shrine
登渡神社 | image = Towatari-jinja_06.jpg | caption = Towatari Shrine's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|36|44.3|N|140|6|22.6|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (formerly [[Myōken]])
[[Kamimusubi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]] | founded_by = [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]] | established = [[1644 (year)|1644]] ([[Shōhō]] 1) | date_destroyed = | location = 3-3-8, Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.towatarijinjya.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = 4–6 September | leadership = }} '''Towatari Shrine''' (登渡神社, ''Towatari-jinja''), also known as '''Nobuto Shrine''' (登戸神社, ''Nobuto-jinja''), is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *[https://www.towatarijinjya.com/ Official website of Towatari Shrine] (Japanese) [[Category:17th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:1600s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiba (city)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Myōken Faith]]{{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:Myōken Faith]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]]

Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Towatari Shrine
登渡神社 | image = Towatari-jinja_06.jpg | caption = Towatari Shrine's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|36|44.3|N|140|6|22.6|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (formerly [[Myōken]])
[[Kamimusubi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]] | founded_by = [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]] | established = [[1644 (year)|1644]] ([[Shōhō]] 1) | date_destroyed = | location = 3-3-8, Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.towatarijinjya.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = 4–6 September | leadership = }} '''Towatari Shrine''' (登渡神社, ''Towatari-jinja''), also known as '''Nobuto Shrine''' (登戸神社, ''Nobuto-jinja''), is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *[https://www.towatarijinjya.com/ Official website of Towatari Shrine] (Japanese) [[Category:17th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:1600s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiba (city)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Myōken Faith]]{{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:Myōken Faith]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]][[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]]

Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Towatari Shrine
登渡神社 | image = Towatari-jinja_06.jpg | caption = Towatari Shrine's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|36|44.3|N|140|6|22.6|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (formerly [[Myōken]])
[[Kamimusubi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]] | founded_by = [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]] | established = [[1644 (year)|1644]] ([[Shōhō]] 1) | date_destroyed = | location = 3-3-8, Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.towatarijinjya.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = 4–6 September | leadership = }} '''Towatari Shrine''' (登渡神社, ''Towatari-jinja''), also known as '''Nobuto Shrine''' (登戸神社, ''Nobuto-jinja''), is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *[https://www.towatarijinjya.com/ Official website of Towatari Shrine] (Japanese) [[Category:17th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:1600s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiba (city)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Myōken Faith]]{{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:Myōken Faith]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:kk]]

[[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Towatari Shrine
登渡神社 | image = Towatari-jinja_06.jpg | caption = Towatari Shrine's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|36|44.3|N|140|6|22.6|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (formerly [[Myōken]])
[[Kamimusubi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]] | founded_by = [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]] | established = [[1644 (year)|1644]] ([[Shōhō]] 1) | date_destroyed = | location = 3-3-8, Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.towatarijinjya.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = 4–6 September | leadership = }} '''Towatari Shrine''' (登渡神社, ''Towatari-jinja''), also known as '''Nobuto Shrine''' (登戸神社, ''Nobuto-jinja''), is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]] | founded_by = [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]] | established = [[1644 (year)|1644]] ([[Shōhō]] 1) | date_destroyed = | location = 3-3-8, Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.towatarijinjya.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = 4–6 September | leadership = }} '''Towatari Shrine''' (登渡神社, ''Towatari-jinja''), also known as '''Nobuto Shrine''' (登戸神社, ''Nobuto-jinja''), is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *[https://www.towatarijinjya.com/ Official website of Towatari Shrine] (Japanese) [[Category:17th-century Shinto shrines]] bunri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[Category:qq--qq]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Myōken Faith]]{{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:Myōken Faith]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Chiba, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Towatari Shrine
登渡神社 | image = Towatari-jinja_06.jpg | caption = Towatari Shrine's ''[[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|36|44.3|N|140|6|22.6|E|region:JP_type:landmark_scale:1000|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (formerly [[Myōken]])
[[Kamimusubi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]
[[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]] | founded_by = [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]] | established = [[1644 (year)|1644]] ([[Shōhō]] 1) | date_destroyed = | location = 3-3-8, Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba]], [[Chiba Prefecture]] | website = {{URL|https://www.towatarijinjya.com/}} | architecture_style = | festival = 4–6 September | leadership = }} '''Towatari Shrine''' (登渡神社, ''Towatari-jinja''), also known as '''Nobuto Shrine''' (登戸神社, ''Nobuto-jinja''), is a [[Shinto]] [[Shinto shrine|shrine]] located in Nobuto, [[Chūō-ku, Chiba|Chūō-ku]], [[Chiba (city)|Chiba City]], [[Chiba Prefecture]], Japan. Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *[https://www.towatarijinjya.com/ Official website of Towatari Shrine] (Japanese) [[Category:17th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:1600s establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Chiba (city)]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Chiba Prefecture]] [[Category:Shinbutsu shūgō]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]‎]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]]]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Myōken Faith]]{{Myōken Faith}}[[Category:Myōken Faith]] [[Category:mass editing for the third subCategory]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:making categorization show up on newly imported categories and templates]] [[Category:1 revision imported]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:Bump-01-21]] [[Category:refreshing imported pages]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]][[Category:qq 6]] [[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:refreshing redirects 1-26]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]][[Category:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:second attempt at refreshing on January 27 2024]] [[Category:kk]][[en:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Towatari Shrine]][[Category:qq]]

[[Kamimusubi]]
[[Takamimusubi]]

Originally a branch [[Buddhist temples in Japan|temple]] of Kongojū-ji, a temple to the [[Buddhism in Japan|Buddhist]] [[Deva (Buddhism)|deity]] [[Myōken]] founded by the [[Chiba clan]] (modern [[Chiba Shrine]]), it was converted into a Shinto shrine during the mid-19th century. ==Deities== The shrine is dedicated to the three deities of creation (造化三神, ''zōka sanshin'') of classical [[Japanese mythology]]: [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]], [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]]. Enshrined together with them is [[:ja:天日鷲神|Ame-no-Hiwashi]], originally the deity of a nearby shrine that was [[Shrine Consolidation Policy|merged]] into Nobuto Shrine in 1908 ([[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] 41).{{cite web|title=登渡神社について|url=https://www.towatarijinjya.com/jinjya|accessdate=2020-11-16|website=Towatari Shrine Official Website|language=Japanese}} ==History== [[File:Bay of Noboto.jpg|left|thumb|270x270px|''Bay of Noboto'' (Nobuto) by [[Katsushika Hokusai]]]] The shrine was originally founded in 1644 ([[Shōhō]] 1) by [[:ja:千葉定胤|Chiba Sadatane]], 33rd head of the [[Chiba clan]], as a Buddhist temple named '''Hakujasan Shinkō-in Jōin-ji''' (白蛇山真光院定胤寺). It was a branch temple or ''matsuji'' (末寺) of Kongojū-ji (also known as Chiba Myōken-ji), a temple dedicated to [[Myōken]], the Buddhist deification of the [[Big Dipper]] and/or the [[Polaris|northern pole star]] revered by the Chiba clan as their patron deity (what is today [[Chiba Shrine]]).{{cite book |editor=Chiba Prefecture |title=千葉県誌 巻上 (Chiba-ken shi, vol. 1) |date=1919 |publisher=Tadaya Shoten |pages=521 |url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/960652/297}}{{cite book |last1=Nozaki |first1=Sabun |title=日本名勝地誌 第二編 (Nihon meishōchi shi, vol. 2) |date=1901 |publisher=Hakubunkan |page=166 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=PkxMpcnBkZQC&dq=%E7%99%BB%E6%B8%A1%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE&pg=PP200}}{{cite web |title=登渡神社 (Towatari-jinja) |url=http://www.jinja.ne.jp/ycDB/Board.cgi/001/db/ycDB-pc-jinja_detail.html?mode:search=1&search:method=1&search:field=jinja_id&search:expr=^$&search:word=1008 |website=神社のひろば 神社探検隊！ |publisher=Chiba-ken Jinjachō |accessdate=2020-11-18|language=Japanese}} In 1867 ([[Keiō]] 3), the temple became a Shinto shrine dedicated to the [[Kami|gods]] [[Ame-no-Minakanushi]] (a deity in Japanese mythology conflated with Myōken), [[Takamimusubi]], and [[Kamimusubi]] and renamed "Towatari Shrine". In 1990 ([[Heisei]] 2), the shrine's ''[[honden]]'' (dating from 1860) was moved to its present location and a new [[Heiden (Shinto)|''hei'']]-''[[Haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' constructed in front of it. ==Structures== *'''''Honden''''' :The ''honden'' was originally Jōin-ji's former [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]], constructed in 1860 by a craftsman named Monjirō (紋次郎) from the [[:ja:八木村 (千葉県)|village of Yagi]] in [[:ja:葛飾郡|Katsushika District]], [[Shimōsa Province]] (part of the modern city of [[Nagareyama]], Chiba Prefecture).{{efn|Other notable projects by this artisan include the former main hall (the current Shaka-dō (釈迦堂) or Hall of [[Gautama Buddha|Śākyamuni]]) of [[Narita-san|Shinshō-ji]] in [[Narita, Chiba|Narita]] (built 1858){{cite book |author1=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |title=成田山通志 (Narita-san Tsushi) |date=1911 |publisher=Shiritsu Narita Toshokan |page=76 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=QgNjpoXOSZMC}} and the former main hall of [[:ja:深川不動堂|Fukagawa Fudō-dō]] (a branch of Shinshō-ji) in [[Fukagawa, Tokyo|Fukagawa]], [[Kōtō|Kōtō City]], [[Tokyo]] - originally a sanctuary dedicated to the [[bodhisattva]] [[Kṣitigarbha]] (地蔵堂, Jizō-dō) in [[:ja:|Ryūfuku-ji]] in what is now [[Inzai, Chiba|Inzai]] built somewhere between 1861 and 1864{{cite web |title=深川不動堂旧本堂 |url=https://www.city.koto.lg.jp/103020/bunkasports/bunka/bunkazaisiseki/kenzobutsu/17348.html |website=Kōtō City Official Website |language=Japanese}}{{cite web |title=今むかし④～本堂移築 |url=http://fukagawafudou.jugem.jp/?eid=314 |website=Narita-san Fukagawa Fudō-dō |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}}.}}{{cite book |editor=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |title=千葉市誌 (Chiba-shi Shi) |date=1958 |publisher=Chiba-shi Shi Hensan Iinkai |page=175}} The bas-reliefs decorating the building were carved by [[:ja:立川和四郎|Tatekawa Washirō Tomimasa]] (立川和四郎富昌, the 2nd generation of the Tatekawa Washirō line of craftsmen) from Suwa District in [[Shinano Province]] (modern [[Nagano Prefecture]]) and/or his son Tomishige (富重).{{cite web |title=小壁嵌板彫刻（市指定文化財） |url=https://www.city.chiba.jp/kyoiku/shogaigakushu/bunkazai/kokabehameita.html |website=Chiba City Official Website |accessdate=2020-11-18 |language=Japanese}} The reliefs were designated [[Tangible Cultural Property (Japan)|tangible cultural properties]] by Chiba City in 1967 ([[Shōwa period|Shōwa]] 42). ==See also== *[[Chiba Shrine]] *[[Chiba clan]] *[[Taira clan]] *[[Shimōsa Province]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{reflist|2}} ==External links== *[https://www.towatarijinjya.com/ Official website of Towatari Shrine] (Japanese) [[Category:17th-century Shinto shrines]]
